coredata.dc:title: Curcumin carbon dots inhibit biofilm formation and expression of esp and gelE genes of Enterococcus faecium
coredata.dc:creator: Sadaqat, Mohammad Hussain
Mobarez, Ashraf Mohabati
Nikkhah, Maryam
coredata.dc:description: The increasing prevalence of vancomycin-resistant Enterococcus faecium, along with the ability of this bacterium to form biofilm on biotic surfaces and medical devices, has created a serious challenge. Therefore, the development of new antibacterial agents is an urgent need. In this study, curcumin carbon dots (Cur-CDs) were synthesized by a one-step hydrothermal method, and its antibacterial and antibiofilm effects were investigated. By broth microdilution method, the minimal inhibitory concentration (MIC) against vancomycin-resistant and sensitive clinical isolates of Enterococcus faecium (two clinical isolates in total) and standard strain of Enterococcus faecalis ATCC 29212 was determined, which were 1000, 1000, and 125 μg/ml, respectively. The inhibitory effect of Cur-CDs on biofilm formation of vancomycin-resistant E. faecium clinical isolates were evaluated by microtiter plate assay. Cur-CDs (1000 μg/ml) significantly prevented (p = 0.009) the biofilm formation of E. faecium isolates. Real-time PCR results showed that Cur-CDs (1000 μg/ml) significantly downregulated the expression of esp and gelE genes (p = 0.001 and p = 000000002, respectively) in clinical isolates of E. faecium, while Cur-CDs did not affect acm gene expression (p = 0.086). This study revealed that Cur-CDs can be effective antibacterial and antibiofilm agents against vancomycin-resistant and biofilm producer E. faecium, which makes them interesting candidates for treating or preventing bacterial infections.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Enterococcus faecium
Antibiofilm
esp
gelE
acm
Gene expression
Curcumin carbon dots

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Effect of Cur-CDs on biofilm formation
Evaluation of expression of biofilm-related genes
The effect of Cur-CDs on the expression of biofilm-related genes

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We are grateful to Mr. Mehdi Asaadi from the Department of Bacteriology and Ms. Marziyeh Mousazadeh from the Nanobiotechnology Department of Tarbiat Modares University for their help during the experiments.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The Synthesis of Cur-CDs by hydrothermal method.
Characterization of Cur-CDs. (A) AFM images and the corresponding size distribution histogram of Cur-CDs. (B) UV–Vis absorption spectrum, (C) Fluorescence spectra, and (D) FTIR spectrum of Cur-CDs.

Determination of the MIC of Cur-CDs against two clinical isolates of E. faecium sensitive and resistant to vancomycin and E. faecalis by broth microdilution method. Columns included Cur-CDs with a final concentration in the range of 1.96–1000 μg ml−1, positive control (Cur-CDs-free), and negative control (bacterial-free). The red circles indicate the MIC obtained for each isolate or strain.

Effect of Cur-CDs at a concentration of 1 mg/ml on the biofilm formation of vancomycin-resistant E. faecium isolates. On the x-axis, the numbers 1 to 5 represent five different vancomycin-resistant E. faecium clinical isolates that could form biofilm. (A) According to the obtained OD - ODc, Cur-CDs significantly reduced the biofilm formation in the treated group compared to the positive control. (B) Biofilm formation inhibition rate in percentage.

Effect of Cur-CDs on the expression of (A) esp, (B) gelE, and (C) acm genes in clinical isolates of vancomycin-resistant E. faecium. On the x-axis, the numbers 1 to 4 represent four vancomycin-resistant E. faecium clinical isolates that could form biofilm and have esp, gelE, and acm genes.
Vancomycin susceptibility of E. faecium isolates.

head.ce:title: Curcumin carbon dots inhibit biofilm formation and expression of esp and gelE genes of Enterococcus faecium

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Bacteriology, Faculty of Medical Science, Tarbiat Modares University, Tehran, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Bacteriology, Faculty of Medical Science, Tarbiat Modares University, Tehran, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Nanobiotechnology, Faculty of Biological Science, Tarbiat Modares University, Tehran, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Nanobiotechnology, Faculty of Biological Science, Tarbiat Modares University, Tehran, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The increasing prevalence of vancomycin-resistant Enterococcus faecium, along with the ability of this bacterium to form biofilm on biotic surfaces and medical devices, has created a serious challenge. Therefore, the development of new antibacterial agents is an urgent need. In this study, curcumin carbon dots (Cur-CDs) were synthesized by a one-step hydrothermal method, and its antibacterial and antibiofilm effects were investigated. By broth microdilution method, the minimal inhibitory concentration (MIC) against vancomycin-resistant and sensitive clinical isolates of Enterococcus faecium (two clinical isolates in total) and standard strain of Enterococcus faecalis ATCC 29212 was determined, which were 1000, 1000, and 125 μg/ml, respectively. The inhibitory effect of Cur-CDs on biofilm formation of vancomycin-resistant E. faecium clinical isolates were evaluated by microtiter plate assay. Cur-CDs (1000 μg/ml) significantly prevented (p = 0.009) the biofilm formation of E. faecium isolates. Real-time PCR results showed that Cur-CDs (1000 μg/ml) significantly downregulated the expression of esp and gelE genes (p = 0.001 and p = 000000002, respectively) in clinical isolates of E. faecium, while Cur-CDs did not affect acm gene expression (p = 0.086). This study revealed that Cur-CDs can be effective antibacterial and antibiofilm agents against vancomycin-resistant and biofilm producer E. faecium, which makes them interesting candidates for treating or preventing bacterial infections.

ce:section.ce:para: Enterococcus faecium along with Enterococcus faecalis is the most common species of Enterococcus causing infections in hospitalized patients [1]. Enterococci, due to their tolerance to heat, chlorine, and a variety of alcohols, can be an explanation for their spread in hospital equipment. Because the prevalence of vancomycin-resistant E. faecium has been increasing from the past to the present, with 80% of E. faecium isolates being vancomycin-resistant, E. faecium has received more attention than ever before [2]. E. faecium can cause catheter-associated urinary tract infections, bacteremia, endocarditis, meningitis, surgical site infections, and peritonitis [3–7]. Vancomycin-resistant enterococcus (VRE) imposes higher hospital costs compared to vancomycin-susceptible enterococcus. On the other hand, the prevalence of VRE is increasing. In Germany, the prevalence of E. faecium resistant to vancomycin increased from 5% to 14.5% between 2001 and 2013 [8]. Also, increasing resistance to vancomycin in bloodstream isolates of E. faecium in Southeast Asia and Eastern Mediterranean countries has been reported at the highest level [9]. In a review study related to Iran, researchers have revealed that the prevalence of antibiotic resistance of E. faecalis and E. faecium increased from 2001 to 2015. Among these two species of enterococci, the antibiotic resistance rate of E. faecium was higher [10].

Biofilms are populations of microbes that are encased in the polymeric matrix and attached to biotic or abiotic surfaces [11]. Biofilm can be 10 to 1000 times more resistant to antibiotics than planktonic bacteria. In addition, the drug resistance of the biofilms is due to the presence of three factors in the biofilms [1]: Extracellular matrix that prevents the penetration of antimicrobial agents [2], Inactivating enzymes of antimicrobial molecules such as catalase and [3] Presence of cells that are metabolically inactive [12]. The bacterial biofilm also causes resistance to phagocytosis, which makes it difficult to eliminate bacteria from the host [13]. Enterococcal infection associated with biofilm has been found in urinary tract infections, gastrointestinal infections, wound infections, and endocarditis [14]. In addition, the formation of enterococcal biofilms has been reported on medical devices such as ureteral and biliary stents, intravascular catheters, and silicone gastrostomy devices [13]. The Esp (enterococcal surface protein), Acm (adhesion of collagen from E. faecium), and GelE (gelatinase) are involved in the formation of E. faecium biofilm and the role of these virulence factors in endocarditis has also been observed [15]. Esp and Acm (encoded by esp and acm, respectively) are LPxTG-type surface proteins that mediate initial attachment in E. faecium, and gelatinase (encoded by gelE) is an extracellular zinc-endopeptidase that hydrolyzes collagen, gelatin, and small peptides [15,16].

Curcumin or diferuloyl methane is a symmetric molecule with the chemical formula C21H20O6 and a molecular weight of 368.38 g mol−1 found in Curcuma longa (turmeric). Curcumin has two aromatic ring systems containing o-methoxy phenolic groups, between which there is a seven carbon linker consisting of an α,β-unsaturated β-diketone moiety [17]. Different biological activities of curcumin such as antioxidant and anticancer activity and also prevention of inflammation by suppressing transcription factors associated with inflammatory responses have been observed [18]. The antibacterial effect of curcumin has been reported through damaging the membrane of various bacteria such as Escherichia coli and Staphylococcus aureus [19]. Inhibition of FtsZ polymerization in Bacillus subtilis and Escherichia coli, inhibition of sortase A and suppression of fibronectin binding in Staphylococcus aureus are other antibacterial activities of curcumin [20–22]. Curcumin also has antibiofilm properties against bacteria, which is achieved by downregulating the expression of quorum sensing genes and inhibiting sortase A [23,24].

Carbon dots are nanomaterials less than 10 nm in size that are composed of hybridized sp2 and sp3 core carbon atoms, as well as functional groups around the core. However, the structure of the carbon dot has not been completely identified. Precursors such as citric acid, ammonium citrate, polyamines, and sugars can be used to synthesize carbon dots. Due to its easy synthesis, water dispersibility, low cytotoxicity, and high biocompatibility, carbon dots have been considered in recent years [25,26]. Carbon dots can be effective against drug-resistant bacteria, intracellular bacteria, and biofilms because of their physicochemical properties, including size and surface charge [27]. Positively charged carbon dots have good antibacterial properties and induce more reactive oxygen species (ROS) production. In addition, negatively charged carbon dots show bacteriostatic properties, while neutral carbon dots display a lower level of antibacterial activity than the previous two carbon dots [26]. Carbon dots also exert their antibacterial effect by producing reactive oxygen species (ROS). ROS are involved in DNA fragmentation and disruption of proteins, lipids, and metabolites [28].

In the present study, we used curcumin as a source for the synthesis of curcumin carbon dots (Cur-CDs) by the one-step hydrothermal method (Fig. 1 ). The Cur-CDs were evaluated for antibacterial and antibiofilm properties as well as their effect on the expression of acm, esp and gelE genes in E. faecium.

Curcumin carbon dots have been synthesized by the one-step hydrothermal method described in the previous study [29], with the difference that pure curcumin has been used as the source instead of turmeric leaves. Briefly, 200 mg of curcumin (Merck, Germany) was mixed with 20 ml of ultrapure water. The mixture was then poured into a Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 10 h. After cooling, the solution was removed from the autoclave and centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 20 min. The yellow supernatant was separated and filtered by a PTFE filter (Membrane Solutions, USA). After that, the obtained solution was dialyzed in ultrapure water through a dialysis membrane (MWCO 500–1000 Da, SpectraPor Float-A-Lyzer, Repligen, US) for 24 h. The purified Cur-CDs were stored at 4 °C for further experiments. To determine the amount of Cur-CDs obtained, the final solution was powdered by a freeze dryer and weighed.

The morphology and size of Cur-CDs were determined by an atomic force microscope (AFM; CP II, Veeco, USA). UV–Visible absorption of Cur-CDs in the range of 215–650 nm was measured by a UV–Visible spectrophotometer (Lambda 25, Perkin Elmer, USA) and its fluorescence spectra were obtained by a fluorescence spectrometer (LS 55, Perkin Elmer, USA). Fluorescence absorptions were taken using a fluorescence spectrometer and the results were analyzed in the wavelength range of 350 to 600. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were taken using an FTIR spectrometer (Nicolet IR 100, Thermo Scientific, USA) and the results were analyzed in the wavenumber range of 4000 to 400 cm−1.

In this study, six clinical isolates of E. faecium archived in the Bacteriology Department of Tarbiat Modares University were used. These isolates were obtained from the urine of hospitalized patients. All the isolates could form biofilm. In terms of susceptibility to vancomycin, one isolate was sensitive and five isolates were resistant to vancomycin. To confirm the identity of the isolates, the housekeeping gene of Enterococcus faecium ddl (Table 1 ) was detected by PCR method [30]. Isolates were stored at −80 °C in tryptic soy broth (TSB; Merck, Germany) with 20% (v/v) glycerol for long-term storage.

Following the CLSI guidelines, the disk diffusion method was used to determine the susceptibility of the isolates to vancomycin. Briefly, the prepared bacterial suspension (1–4 x 108 CFU ml−1) was inoculated on Mueller-Hinton agar medium (Merck, Germany) and a 30 μg vancomycin disk (Liofilchem, Italy) was placed on the agar surface. After 24 h of incubation at 35 °C, the susceptibility of the isolates to vancomycin was detected according to the CLSI guidelines by examining the diameter of the growth inhibition zone. S. aureus ATCC 25923 was used as a quality control strain [31].

Minimal inhibitory concentrations (MICs) of Cur-CDs against two clinical isolates of vancomycin-sensitive E. faecium and resistant to vancomycin and E. faecalis ATCC 29212 were determined by broth microdilution method. Thus, Cur-CDs were serially diluted in Mueller Hinton broth (Merck, Germany) inside a 96-well microtiter plate. The prepared bacterial suspension was then added to the wells so that the wells had a concentration of 5 × 105 CFU ml−1 of bacteria and a final concentration of Cur-CDs in the range of 1.96–1000 μg ml−1. One column of the plate was used as a bacterial growth control (Cur-CDs-free) and one column was used as a negative control (bacterial-free). The plate was incubated at 35 °C for 20 h. The MIC was defined as the lowest concentration of Cur-CDs that inhibited the visible growth of bacteria [32,33].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of Cur-CDs on biofilm formation
ce:section.ce:para:
The effect of Cur-CDs on the biofilm formation of vancomycin-resistant E. faecium clinical isolates (These isolates could form a biofilm that was previously detected) was determined by microtiter plate assay as previously described with some modifications [34]. Briefly, 180 μl of TSB with 1.5% glucose was added to the flat-bottomed polystyrene 96-well microtiter plate. Then, 20 μl of bacterial suspension (1.5
×
108 CFU ml−1) and 100 μl of Cur-CDs at a concentration of 3 mg ml−1 were added to the wells so that the final concentration of Cur-CDs was 1000 μg ml−1. In addition, one group was used as a positive control (bacterial culture without Cur-CDs) and three wells were used as a negative control (TSB with 1.5% glucose only) in the microplate. After incubation at 35 °C for 12 h, the contents of the wells were removed and the wells were washed three times with phosphate-buffered saline (PBS). Next, the bacteria attached to the microplate were fixed with methanol for 20 min and then stained with 0.5% crystal violet. After 15 min, the crystal violet was removed from the microplate and 95% ethanol was added to the wells. The microplate was incubated at room temperature for 30 min to dissolve the dye bound to the biofilms by ethanol. By a Multiskan 352 microplate reader (Labsystems, Finland), the optical density (OD) of each well was measured at 492 nm. Staphylococcus epidermidis ATCC 35984 was used as a control strain for biofilm formation. The experiment was performed in triplicate. Optical density cut-off (ODc) [35] and inhibition of biofilm formation as a percentage were calculated as follows:
O D c =
(
3 × s t a n d a r d
d e v i a t i o n
o f
n e g a t i v e
c o n t r o l
)
+ a v e r a g e
O D
o f
n e g a t i v e
c o n t r o l
i n h i b i t i o n
o f
b i o f i l m
f o r m a t i o n % =
(
1 −
O D
o f
t h e
s a m p l e − O D c
a v e r a g e
(
O D
o f
t h e
p o s i t i v e
c o n t r o l − O D c
)
)
× 100

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of expression of biofilm-related genes
ce:section.ce:para: Isolates that were resistant to vancomycin and formed biofilms and also had genes related to biofilms (esp, gelE and acm genes that were previously detected by PCR method) were used to study gene expression. briefly, 50 μl of the bacterial suspension was mixed with 450 μl of TSB medium containing 1.5% glucose in a tube. Then 500 μl of Cur-CDs was added at a concentration of 2 mg ml−1 (final concentration: 1000 μg ml−1). Also, a group of bacterial cultures without Cur-CDs was considered as a control. After incubation at 35 °C for 12 h, the tubes were centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min. The supernatant was then removed and RNA extraction from the bacterial pellet was performed by Hybrid-R™ RNA purification kit (GeneAll, South Korea). By using the Easy™ cDNA Synthesis Kit (Parstous, Iran), the extracted RNA was converted to single-stranded cDNA. The qRT-PCR was performed using RealQ Plus 2x Master Mix Green (AMPLIQON, Denmark) in a Rotor-Gene® Q (Qiagen, Germany). Primers from previous studies (Table 1) were used for qRT-PCR and 16s rRNA was determined as a reference. The assay was performed in triplicate (A total of 12 RNA samples from the treated group and 12 RNA samples from the untreated group related to 4 clinical isolates of E. faecium) and the Pfaffl equation was used to calculate relative gene expression [36]. The Pfaffl equation is given below:

In this study, data with error bars as mean ± standard deviation are reported by OriginPro 2019b. Shapiro-Wilk test was used to examine the normal distribution of data and Mann-Whitney and t-test in SPSS 26 software were used for statistical analysis. p-value < 0.05 was considered statistically significant.

The AFM data and images showed that Cur-CDs were spherical with an average size of 1.178 nm (Fig. 2 A). The UV–Vis spectroscopy of Cur-CDs showed an absorption peak at 283 nm that corresponds to the π-π* transition of the CC bond (Fig. 2B). The Cur-CDs exhibited a maximum emission peak at 454 nm when excited at 370 nm (Fig. 2C). As the excitation wavelength increased, the emission peak shifted to longer wavelengths. Also, with increasing excitation wavelength up to 370 nm, the emission intensity of Cur-CDs increased and then decreased (390 and 410 nm). The FTIR spectrum at wavenumber 3421 cm−1 showed a broad peak corresponding to O–H stretching vibration in the hydroxyl functional group (Fig. 2D). Peaks related to C–H stretching vibration and C–H bending vibration of the methyl group are located at 2924 and 1384 cm−1, respectively. The peak at 1637 cm−1 belongs to the CC stretching vibration, which indicates the formation of the graphitic carbon core of Cur-CDs. Also, the C–O–C stretching vibration was exhibited by a peak at 1140 cm−1.

According to the CLSI guideline [41], because the zone diameters of 5 isolates of E. faecium were less than 14 mm, these isolates were reported to be resistant to vancomycin. One isolate also had an inhibition zone diameter of 18 mm, so it was sensitive to vancomycin (Table 2 ).

The MIC of Cur-CDs against two vancomycin-resistant and sensitive clinical isolates of E. faecium was 1000 μg ml−1, and no difference was observed in these two isolates. However, Cur-CDs with a lower concentration of 125 μg ml−1 were able to prevent the visible growth of the standard strain of Enterococcus faecalis ATCC 29212 (Fig. 3 ).

The antibiofilm activity of Cur-CDs at a concentration of 1000 μg ml−1 against five clinical isolates of vancomycin-resistant E. faecium was evaluated by microtiter plate assay. As shown in Fig. 4 A, Cur-CDs at a concentration of 1 mg ml−1 were able to significantly inhibit (p = 0.009) biofilm formation compared to positive control. Among the isolates, the highest inhibition of biofilm formation by Cur-CDs was 99.4% and the lowest was 79% (Fig. 4B).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effect of Cur-CDs on the expression of biofilm-related genes
ce:section.ce:para: The qRT-PCR method was used to evaluate the effect of Cur-CDs at a concentration of 1000 μg ml−1 on the expression of biofilm-related genes in clinical isolates of vancomycin-resistant E. faecium. Cur-CDs significantly downregulated the expression of esp and gelE genes compared to untreated control (p = 0.001 and p = 000000002, respectively) (Fig. 5 A and B). However, Cur-CDs did not significantly downregulate acm gene expression (p = 0.086) (Fig. 5C).

The ability of E. faecium to form biofilm on biotic surfaces and medical devices, along with the increasing prevalence of vancomycin resistance related to this bacterium, is an urgent issue. Therefore, finding new antibacterial agents is a priority.

In this study, Cur-CDs were synthesized by hydrothermal method, and their antibacterial and antibiofilm effects on clinical isolates of E. faecium were investigated. Approximately similar to the study by Saravanan et al. [29], the Cur-CDs were spherical with an average size of 1.178 nm. The small size enables carbon dots to penetrate the bacterial biofilm or inhibit biofilm formation [42]. According to previous studies, carbon dots derived from gentamicin sulfate and tinidazole can better penetrate the biofilm and kill bacteria due to their size (2–8 nm and 16.5 nm, respectively) compared to raw antibiotics [43,44]. The FTIR and UV–Vis spectroscopy results showed the presence of a CC bond, which is evidence of the graphitic structure of the Cur-CDs core. The absence of C–H peaks in FTIR results indicates complete carbonization of the carbon source in the process of carbon dots synthesis at high temperatures (formation of the graphitic core only) and the lack of functional groups on the carbon dots surface [45]. However, in this study, C–H peaks were exhibited in the FTIR results, which is the reason for incomplete carbonization and consequently the presence of functional groups on the Cur-CDs surface. The importance of functional groups such as the hydroxyl group (present in the FTIR result of this study) for their antibacterial effects has been noted in previous studies [26,46]. Also, the peak of C–O–C stretching vibration indicates intermolecular dehydration and polymerization in the process of Cur-CDs synthesis [47,48].

In the present study, the MIC of Cur-CDs against clinical isolates of E. faecium resistant and sensitive to vancomycin was 1000 μg ml−1 and against the standard strain of E. faecalis ATCC 29212 was 125 μg ml−1. Also, Cur-CDs at 1000 μg ml−1 significantly inhibited (p = 0.009) the formation of biofilm of vancomycin-resistant E. faecium clinical isolates compared with positive control. Porcaro et al. reported that AgNPs-3MPS-TG1 at 59 μg ml−1 reduced growth of the standard strain of E. faecalis ATCC 29212 by 90%, while AgNPs-3MPS and AgNPs-3MPS-TG2 at the tested concentrations (<128 μg ml−1) did not have a 90% inhibitory effect [49]. In the study by Jose et al. the MIC of Li–Ag/Zn/CSNC nanocomposite against the standard strain of E. faecalis ATCC 29212 was 62.5 mg ml−1 [50]. Moreover, the MIC of nanocurcumin and carbon dots obtained from turmeric leaves against different standard strains of gram-positive and gram-negative were reported to be 75–250 μg ml−1 and 250–1000 μg ml−1, respectively [29,51]. Compared to the mentioned studies, if the antibacterial activity against standard strains (not clinical isolates) is the basis of comparison, Cur-CDs need a lower concentration to inhibit bacterial growth than AgNPs-3MPS, AgNPs-3MPS-TG2, Li–Ag/Zn/CSNC and carbon dots obtained from turmeric leaves. Therefore, Cur-CDs have better antibacterial activity than these four nanoparticles. Also, Cur-CDs showed a weaker antibacterial effect than AgNPs-3MPS-TG1 and almost the same antibacterial effect as nanocurcumin. On the other hand, it is important to note that according to a previous report, silver nanoparticles cause toxic effects on humans and the environment, which is one of the disadvantages of these nanoparticles [52]. Singh et al. synthesized curcumin quantum dots (CurQDs) by a two-step bottom up wet milling method. The MIC of CurQDs against different bacteria has been reported in the range of 3.91–7.825 μg ml−1 and also this type of quantum dots in the concentration of 0.25 μg ml−1 inhibited the formation of biofilm of different bacteria [53]. In a study, carbon dots from Lactobacillus plantarum were synthesized by hydrothermal method, which at 1 mg ml−1 reduced the biofilm formation of E. coli by almost 90% (similar to the present study in terms of concentration and inhibitory effect), while they did not affect the growth of E. coli [25]. Overall, Cur-CDs exhibited weaker antibacterial and antibiofilm activity compared to CurQD based on the reported concentrations. Compared to carbon dots obtained from Lactobacillus plantarum, Cur-CDs had a better antibacterial effect and a similar antibiofilm effect. The remarkable effect of CurQDs may be due to their synthesis method (two-step bottom up wet milling method). In previous studies, the role of Esp, Acm, and GelE in the development of endocarditis and biofilm formation in E. faecium has been mentioned [5,15,38,54–56].

In this study, Cur-CDs significantly downregulated the expression of esp and gelE genes (p = 0.001 and p = 000000002, respectively), while Cur-CDs did not affect the expression of acm (p = 0.086). In the study of Partoazar et al. NanoZnO/Ze (nanozinc oxide/zeolite) at concentrations of 10–40 mg ml−1 inhibited the formation of E. faecalis biofilm. Also, NanoZnO/Ze at 10 mg ml−1 downregulated the expression of the esp gene in E. faecalis [57]. Generally, Cur-CDs were more effective compared to NanoZnO/Ze considering the concentration used. According to the results of this study, the downregulation of esp and gelE genes expression by Cur-CDs may play a role in inhibiting E. faecium biofilm formation. More studies are needed to confirm this hypothesis because genes and other factors are also involved in the formation of E. faecium biofilm.

This study revealed that Cur-CDs, in addition to their antibacterial activity, can inhibit biofilm formation and the expression of esp and gelE genes of vancomycin-resistant E. faecium. Therefore, these properties make Cur-CDs interesting candidates for treating or preventing E. faecium infection in humans. However, further studies are needed to discover new insights into the antibacterial effect of Cur-CDs against different Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria isolated from human specimens.

Mohammad Hussain Sadaqat: Writing – original draft, Software, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Ashraf Mohabati Mobarez: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Conceptualization. Maryam Nikkhah: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Validation, Methodology, Data curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We are grateful to Mr. Mehdi Asaadi from the Department of Bacteriology and Ms. Marziyeh Mousazadeh from the Nanobiotechnology Department of Tarbiat Modares University for their help during the experiments.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Transition of Enterococcus faecium from commensal organism to nosocomial pathogen

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Willems RJ, van Schaik W. Transition of Enterococcus faecium from commensal organism to nosocomial pathogen. Future microbiology. 2009;4(9):1125-1135.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The rise of the Enterococcus: beyond vancomycin resistance

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Treatment of vancomycin-resistant Enterococcus faecium infections

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Lai KK. Treatment of vancomycin-resistant Enterococcus faecium infections. Archives of internal medicine. 1996;156(22):2579-2584.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Contribution of the enterococcal surface protein Esp to pathogenesis of Enterococcus faecium endocarditis

Infection control and hospital epidemiology. 2016;37(11):1288-1301.
Fiore E, Van Tyne D, Gilmore MS. Pathogenicity of Enterococci. Microbiology spectrum. 2019;7(4):10.1128/microbiolspec.GPP3-0053-2018.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Economic burden of nosocomial infections caused by vancomycin-resistant enterococci

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Global prevalence of antibiotic resistance in blood-isolated Enterococcus faecalis and Enterococcus faecium: a systematic review and meta-analysis
